event: Saint John the Baptist in the Wilderness
symbols: pointing finger — John's role as the one who points to Christ: 'Behold the Lamb of God'
staff or reed cross — the prophetic authority and the cross John proclaimed was coming
animal-skin garment — the ascetic wilderness life and identification with Elijah
halo — sanctity and John's unique role as the greatest born of women
wilderness landscape — the desert of preparation where John preached repentance
description: A full-length standing figure of a young man with long flowing hair and a golden halo. He wears a green undergarment partially covered by a pink-mauve mantle, suggestive of camel skin or rough fabric. He holds a tall thin staff or reed cross in one hand and raises the other in a pointing gesture. The figure stands in a rocky landscape with sparse trees visible in the background. The panel painting shows some wear and aging, with cracking visible across the surface.
theological_description: Botticini's John the Baptist presents the forerunner of Christ in the wilderness where he was called to preach. Luke records: 'The word of God came to John the son of Zechariah in the wilderness. And he went into all the region around the Jordan, proclaiming a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins' (Luke 3:2-3). Matthew describes his appearance: 'John wore a garment of camel's hair and a leather belt around his waist, and his food was locusts and wild honey' (Matthew 3:4), identifying him with Elijah (2 Kings 1:8). The pointing gesture is John's defining act — directing all attention away from himself toward Christ: 'The next day he saw Jesus coming toward him, and said, Behold, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world!' (John 1:29). John understood his role perfectly: 'He must increase, but I must decrease' (John 3:30). Jesus declared him the greatest of the prophets: 'Among those born of women there has arisen no one greater than John the Baptist' (Matthew 11:11). John fulfilled the prophecy of Isaiah: 'The voice of one crying in the wilderness: Prepare the way of the Lord, make his paths straight' (Isaiah 40:3, Matthew 3:3).

scripture_refs.reason: John's declaration 'Behold the Lamb of God' — his pointing gesture directs all to Christ
John preaching in the wilderness wearing camel's hair — the setting and appearance depicted
The voice crying in the wilderness to prepare the Lord's way — John's prophetic identity
The greatest born of women — John's unique sanctity represented by his halo
He must increase, I must decrease — John's role as pointer rather than destination
